Discord Quick Start: A Hands-on Walkthrough of the Community App
I've been migrating over to Discord lately — I used to basically live on Slack — so here's a quick walkthrough on the basics of Discord.
Discord's left sidebar splits into two main parts:
If you've already joined some servers, you'll see their icons in the server list. For me, that's the Hexschool icon.
Home is basically your personal home view, and the features here are pretty simple — easier to just show you the picture.
On the left there's an activity feed showing what your friends are up to, but you'll only see it if you're in the same server as them.
Just like Slack, Discord lets you spin up an ad-hoc group chat. Click "New Group DM" at the top of your home view and drag your friends in.
" button on the left to do the same thing.
Heads up: group DMs are capped at 10 people. If you need more than that, just spin up a server instead (there's no limit on creating servers).
How do you add friends? It's straightforward — click your avatar at the bottom and you can copy your username tag.
Send it to whoever you want to friend, then they paste it into "Add Friend" and they're added.

Now onto servers. Each server has its own feature set, so I won't go into every server's specifics, but I'll cover the basics. Pick any server to jump in — I'll use the Hexschool server as the example.
Heads up: one account can join at most 200 servers (not that anyone normal would actually join that many).
Each one has its own icon.
Just click into a text channel to start chatting. Pretty straightforward.
If you don't want constant pings, hit the bell icon at the top to mute notifications.
Voice channels are a bit different — just click in and you're immediately in the call.
To leave, click "Disconnect" at the bottom.
Make sure your mic is actually on if you want to talk.
You can also do video and screen share. On a regular server, screen share caps at 720p.
Your friends can click your avatar and hit "Watch Stream" to view your screen.
These are mostly for server announcements, so regular users usually can't chat in them.
Honestly, I've never used these, so I can't say much.
Threads are a more recent Discord feature — they're like Slack threads. Find the message you want to spin a discussion off, and hit "Create Thread".
You'll need to pick a thread name and how long the thread stays open without activity before auto-closing.
After creating it, the original message gets this little badge underneath:
Once you join a thread, it shows up in your left sidebar.
Closing a thread doesn't delete the messages — it just temporarily hides it from your sidebar. You can also leave it early by clicking "Leave Thread".
Discord also has a built-in way to view active vs closed threads — it's at the top of the channel.
You can tell a private channel because there's a lock icon next to the channel name.
A lot of people get tripped up on how to check their roles. The bottom of the screen is your personal panel.
To see your roles in the current server, search for yourself in the member list on the right.
Once you find yourself, click your avatar to see your roles in that server.
If that's too much effort, just type something in any text channel and then click your own avatar — same result.

Discord has a ton of features, way too many to cover all in one post — I could probably write a whole 30-day series on it. So that's the basics; you can explore the rest on your own.

I've been moving over to Discord lately — I used to live on Slack — so I figured I'd write a basic Discord usage walkthrough covering the home view, servers, channels, threads, and a few other essentials.
